data.title: ECB's baseline is materialising on energy markets, ECB policymaker Escriva says
data.description:
The European Central Bank's baseline scenario for the economy, which envisages a short-lived impact from the Iran war, is materialising on the energy market, ECB policymaker José Luis Escrivá said on Wednesday.

"When you look at these scenarios and you compare with market prices, oil and future prices and gas prices as well, actually, the central scenario is materialising very closely over the last six weeks," the Spanish central bank governor said.

data.title: US natural gas stockpiles likely rose by 51 billion cubic feet last week
data.description:
U.S. energy firms likely added 51 billion cubic feet of natural gas into storage last week, according to the average estimate in a Reuters poll released on Wednesday.

The estimate compares with an injection of 22 bcf during the same week a year ago and an average increase of 38 bcf over the past five years (2021-2025) for the week.

In the prior week ended April 3, utilities added 50 bcf of gas into storage. (USOILN=ECI)

If correct, the forecast for the week ended April 10 would increase stockpiles to 1.962 trillion cubic feet. That would be about 6% above the same week a year ago and about 5% above the five-year average for the week.

The U.S. Energy Information Administration is scheduled to release its weekly storage report at 10:30 a.m. EDT (1430 GMT) on Thursday.

There were about 68 heating degree days last week, compared with a 30-year normal of 74 for the period, data from financial firm LSEG showed.

HDDs measure the number of degrees a day's average temperature is below 65 degrees Fahrenheit (18 degrees Celsius) to estimate demand to heat homes and businesses.

Reuters polled 10 analysts, whose estimates ranged from injections of 40 bcf to 62 bcf, with a median increase of 51 bcf.

Early estimates for the week ending April 17 ranged from injections of 65 bcf to 91 bcf, with an average increase of 86 bcf.

Those figures compare with an injection of 77 bcf during the same week last year and a five-year average increase of 64 bcf.

Following is a list of poll participants. All figures are in billion cubic feet:

Organization

Forecasts in bcf

Natural Gas Intelligence

62

Energy Aspects

59

Energy Ventures Analysis

56

53

data.title: Mosaic ImmunoEngineering 10-K: Net loss $(687,846), revenue not reported, EPS not disclosed
data.description:
Mosaic ImmunoEngineering reported a net loss of $(687,846) for the year ended December 31, 2025, while the filing did not present revenue or earnings-per-share figures for the period. The company's 10-K discloses limited financial detail and emphasizes constrained near-term operations driven by minimal cash and planned financing efforts. Management implemented cost and headcount reductions in 2025 to preserve limited research activity while seeking additional capital.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Income: $(687,846) net loss for the year ended Dec 31, 2025 (per MD&A).
  • Operating Income: Not reported (no operating income/expense subtotal presented separately in Item 7).
  • Revenue: Not reported (no revenue or sales disclosed in Item 7/Item 8).
  • Net Income Per Share: Not reported (no EPS or per-share amount disclosed for year ended Dec 31, 2025).

Business Highlights

  • Corporate transactions: Completed a reverse merger in August 2020 that consolidated Private Mosaic as the operating entity and refocused corporate strategy.
  • R&D focus and pipeline: Development-stage biotech advancing immunotherapies; historically pursued a CPMV license, with the Case Western Reserve University license terminated in March 2024.
  • Partnerships and term sheets: Entered a term sheet with Oncotelic in April 2024 for necroptosis vascular disrupting agents (VDAs) and AI access; the binding term sheet expired in June 2025.
  • Cost and headcount management: Implemented reductions in 2025 that lowered R&D and G&A payroll costs while preserving limited research activity.
  • Liquidity-driven outlook: Near-term operations are constrained by minimal cash on hand; management plans to pursue equity or debt financings to fund ongoing development.

Original SEC Filing: Mosaic ImmunoEngineering Inc. [ CPMV ] - 10-K - Apr. 15, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Union Pacific Signs 7-Year Deal with Rocky Mountain Steel Mills
data.description:
By Christopher Kuo

Union Pacific Railroad has agreed to a seven-year contract to use steel rail from Rocky Mountain Steel Mills.

Union Pacific said the partnership underscores its commitment to buying steel from domestic manufacturers. It has used steel rail from Rocky Mountain Steel since the early 1890s, the company said.

Rocky Mountain Steel, a Colorado-based steel-rail manufacturer, expects to begin operations at a new rail mill this year, funded by more than $1 billion in investment, the companies said. The mill will be powered by an 1,800-acre solar farm, they said.

The new pact between the companies ends pending legal disputes between them, the companies said. Union Pacific has withdrawn its November lawsuit against Rocky Mountain Steel, they added.

"As Union Pacific pursues its merger with Norfolk Southern to create the nation's first transcontinental railroad, our ability to partner with Rocky Mountain Steel becomes even more critical as our merger is positioned to strengthen the nation's manufacturing core as well as domestic job growth," said Union Pacific Chief Executive Jim Vena.

Write to Christopher Kuo at chris.kuo@wsj.com

data.title: Emerging-Market Pioneer Mark Mobius Dies at 89 — WSJ
data.description:
By Vicky Ge Huang

Emerging-markets guru and evangelist Mark Mobius has died at the age of 89, according to a statement from his LinkedIn page.

Mobius joined the money manager Franklin Templeton in 1987 to launch one of the first funds dedicated to emerging markets. His star rose during the ensuing bull market, earning him a reputation as the "Indiana Jones of emerging-market investing" and the "godfather of emerging markets."

Mobius retired from Franklin Templeton in 2018 after more than three decades with the firm. Under his leadership, what began as a $100 million in the Templeton Emerging Markets Fund blossomed into the firm's sprawling Emerging Markets Group. By the time he left the firm, the group managed over $40 billion across 70 countries, according to his official website.

He remained active in the emerging-markets sector after his retirement, managing the Mobius Emerging Opportunities Fund since June 2024. Following his death, John Ninia and Eric Nguyen, both partners at Mobius Investments, will assume leadership responsibilities, according to the LinkedIn post.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Bessent says he is optimistic Warsh will be Fed chair 'on time'
data.description:
U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Wednesday said he was optimistic that Kevin Warsh, President Donald Trump's nominee for Federal Reserve Chair, would assume the post on time.

"He is going to have a hearing on the 21st of this month. He's a great candidate. We know that the Republicans on the Senate Banking Committee are aligned in that," Bessent told a White House briefing.

"I am very optimistic that Kevin Warsh will be the Chair of the Fed on time," he said.

data.title: Nvidia Stock Heads for 11-Day Winning Streak. What It Needs to Really Break Out. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark

Nvidia is on a hot streak. But investors waiting for a decisive break out of the chip maker's recent range might still need to be patient.

Nvidia shares were up 1.1% at $198.75 in morning trading. If the move holds, it will mark 11 consecutive days of gains, its longest winning streak on record based on data going back to 1999, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

The rally has been helpful but still not the breakout to new highs that shareholders have been waiting on since a drop back from levels of more than $200 late last year and which Wall Street targets imply.

The next big hope is from Big Tech earnings, kicking off later this month. Nvidia investors will want continued increases in capital expenditure. More than that, they would like to specifically hear that new leading artificial-intelligence models are being trained on Nvidia's current-generation Blackwell hardware.

The competition point is a sore one at the moment, with multiple AI chip rivals announcing deals. Broadcom has just extended its deal with Meta Platforms, while Amazon.com has talked of selling more of its own custom AI chips to third parties.

However, Wall Street is generally confident that Nvidia will continue to be the leader in AI chips as the sector begins to focus on inference — generating output from AI models — rather than training.

"As long as the larger hyperscalers continue to buy in quantity and acknowledge that it is a superior product, Nvidia remains in a class separate from other semiconductors," said Brian Mulberry, chief market strategist at Zacks Investment Management.

"That does represent a certain amount of market share that could be at risk to new technology/processes (i.e. quantum computing) but in the near term there is no evidence of Nvidia losing that share of the market."

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: The Secret Techniques of a 150-Year-Old Soy Sauce Maker — WSJ
data.description:
By Setareh Baig and Kosaku Narioka | Photography and video by Fumi Nagasaka for WSJ. Magazine

Yasuo Yamamoto grew up playing hide-and-seek between old barrels in the storehouse where his family has been brewing soy sauce for more than 150 years. He's one of the last brewers to use an ancient method of fermenting in wooden vats.

"People probably think soy sauce is salty," Yamamoto, 53, says in Japanese. In his rare blend, which ages for four years, umami lingers, not saltiness, and the barrels' microorganisms lend to the complex taste. He lives on-site, working 365 days a year running the Yamaroku Soy Sauce brewing house on the hilly Shodoshima Island in Japan. "It's actually fun to do," he says.

Yamamoto lives with his wife, Mayumi, with whom he shares two sons, 20 and 22, and a daughter, 18. Here, translated into English, he discusses his stint working in Tokyo, how he safeguards part of a UNESCO food tradition and whether his kids will inherit the family business.

Can you walk me through your process of making soy sauce?

We steam the soybeans and roast and crush the wheat. We bring in the koji, and then prepare the mash in wooden vats during the winter. Then we let it ferment and age to make soy sauce. The main product is saishikomi [twice-brewed] soy sauce, so we take soy sauce that has been squeezed after one year, return it to the vat, add koji to it once again, and let it ferment and age, taking a total of four years. We press that mash, sterilize it by heating, bottle it and ship it out.

What was it like for you to grow up in the family business?

When I was little, I didn't really realize that my family ran a soy sauce brewery. I used to wonder why my dad was always at home when all my friends' fathers went off to work. It was only around the fourth or fifth grade of elementary school that I finally realized, "Wait, maybe we're a soy sauce shop."

Who has had the greatest influence on your work?

That is definitely my father. There were two things he was always saying. He kept saying that our soy sauce couldn't be made without this storehouse, its earthen walls, earthen floors and wooden vats. And he was always saying that he wanted to make delicious soy sauce.

What kind of values have been passed down in your family?

We were a family that didn't eat out. Until I went to college, I had only eaten out twice in my life, in 18 years.

Was that because of some particular preference of your father's?

No, probably just because we didn't have money.

Did you always think you would inherit the family business?

I had no intention of taking over the business, and my father told me that I didn't have to. He told me that I didn't need to take it over because soy sauce didn't make much money.

What was the catalyst for that change?

I got a job in sales at a local tsukudani manufacturer. When I was doing sales in Tokyo, I used to visit high-end supermarkets like Kinokuniya and Miuraya. So, I went back home thinking that this might actually work out.

What are the benefits of brewing in wooden barrels?

To be honest, it's delicious. Just like with wine or whiskey, fermenting and aging in wooden containers creates a flavor and aroma unique to that specific brewery, and the taste and scent become complex.

Have you had any discussions with your children about taking over the family business?

I want them to take over the business. Another soy sauce maker told me, "The soul of a 3-year-old stays with them until they are 100" [a popular Japanese proverb], so I should imprint it in their brains by the time they are 3. When they were small, I used to whisper about it in their ears.

About a year and a half ago, when I asked my daughter if she would work at Yamaroku Soy Sauce, she told me that if she had nothing else to do, she might work here, but the world is a big place. None of the three have any intention of taking over. But if you asked me whether I had any intention of inheriting it when I was around 20, I didn't have the slightest inclination either. I hope that after they've worked for five or 10 years, one of them might take over.

Why do you think wooden-vat brewing is a method worth preserving?

Even though Washoku [traditional Japanese cuisine] has become a [UNESCO-recognized cultural] heritage, the basic seasonings are industrial products. Since I think soy sauce is definitely the most fundamental of Washoku's basic seasonings, I believe we must preserve the traditional method for the next generation.

You mentioned that when your father told you that you didn't have to take over, the family business wasn't very profitable.

It was almost a miracle they hadn't gone under.

Compared to back then, have things become a little more financially secure?

Exports actually make up one-third of our sales. Overseas exports, particularly to the U.S., are substantial. There are more people in the U.S. who understand the value of our soy sauce than there are in Japan.

This interview has been edited and condensed for clarity.

Write to Setareh Baig at setareh.baig@wsj.com and Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com

data.title: How to Wear Long Shorts (Without Looking Frumpy) — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Fashion Team | Photography by Elizabeth Arvelos Coetzee

Knee-length shorts are to men what mom jeans once were to women: a versatile wardrobe option often labeled as dowdy. But trust us: when they're pleated, tailored and in a nice fabric, long shorts are a polished yet unexpected silhouette that is perfect for any season where the weather changes on the dime. Be sure to get them tailored to just at the knee — not too far past — then dress them up for a cocktail party or down for weekend brunch. See four ways below.

The Transitional Hero

The key to navigating the in-between seasons? Layers and a combination of proportions. Long shorts can be a great way to navigate the days that start off cold then warm up (or vice versa). The cropped jacket balances out the silhouette.

The Wardrobe Remix

Odds are, you have a tank top, button-down and sandals in your closet. Hold these staples close while experimenting with a pleated pair of shorts in a cotton-linen blend. It's an elevated take on a classic dadcore look.

The Perfect Pair

Sweater and shorts is as classic a combination as peanut butter and jelly. Zhuzh it up by choosing dark gray shorts and swapping out the college sweatshirt for a polo and henley.

The Matching Set

When you think of a warm-weather suit, linen probably springs to mind. It's a great option, but it's also worth trying a tonal matching short-set in a suiting fabric. Try it out the next time you're wondering what "cocktail attire" means.

data.title: Great Moments in American Advertising History — Journal Report
data.description:
By Jef I. Richards

"USA250: The Story of the World's Greatest Economy" is a yearlong WSJ series examining America's first 250 years. Read more about it from Editor in Chief Emma Tucker.

Advertising didn't start in the U.S. In fact, it goes back at least 2,500 years: An ancient vase in the Louvre carries a notice that reads, "Buy me and you'll get a bargain." But as the U.S. grew, it helped turn sales pitches into an art — and an industry.

Here's a look at some of the big moments in America's long and profitable relationship with sales pitches.

Born in Boston

America broke into the ad business in 1704, with the birth of the Boston News-Letter. Not only was it the first newspaper in the colonies with any staying power, it was the first to run advertisements. Among the alerts about real estate for sale and a reward for apprehending a clothing thief, you'll find a grim reminder of the realities of the time: an ad offering slaves. The trade would feature prominently in American advertising for decades to come, as did pitches for often-dubious cures known as patent medicine — the proverbial snake oil.

An ad revolution

The next big ad innovation came from a man who cranked out ideas by the dozen: Ben Franklin. When he took over the Pennsylvania Gazette in 1729, he experimented with the look and layouts of ads, using larger headlines, fancy borders and illustrations.

Advertising even played a role in colonial discontent, and then the Revolution. In 1765, when Britain tried to enforce onerous fees on newspaper ads, "Sons of Liberty" groups rose up to oppose it.

Over the next decade, rebellious colonists used ads to spread the word about boycotts on British products. Founding father Sam Adams ran one notice saying: "WILLIAM JACKSON, an IMPORTER. . . . It is desired that the SONS and DAUGHTERS of LIBERTY, wouldn't buy any one thing of him, for in so doing they will bring Disgrace upon themselves, and their Posterity, for ever and ever, AMEN."

George Washington, meanwhile, used advertising to recruit soldiers. (He was also susceptible to sales pitches himself: Just before he became president, he ordered cloth for a suit based on an ad he had seen.)

A branding boom

As the new nation entered a new century, advertising kept evolving. Even the legendary pitchman Paul Revere decided he could reach more people in print than on horseback, turning to newspaper ads to pitch his foundry.

Then came another upheaval that rocked the ad business: the Industrial Revolution. America was concocting tons of new inventions, and they needed ads to spread the word, like for Benjamin Babbitt's bar soap.

Ads, in fact, quickly turned Babbitt into a widely recognized brand. And it wasn't the only company that got that kind of boost: The concept of "branding" took off as mass production and distribution, among other changes, made it crucial for businesses to stand out. Thanks to ads, you no longer just bought "flour," you bought a brand like King Arthur Flour. You didn't just choose a generic bicycle in the late 1880s, you went for a distinct brand, like the Psycho bicycle.

Photography and new printing methods helped ads evolve, and more competition led to more experimentation. In one short-lived ad strategies, sellers photographed women dressed in merchandise, usually holding a banner with the seller's name on it. (Hence the nickname: "banner ladies.")

Over the airwaves

It took a few decades for advertising to take hold in radio — the first commercial didn't go out over the airwaves until 1920 — but it was tied to TV from the start. The first real TV sales pitch, which ran before a Dodgers-Phillies game in 1941, was primitive: a 10-second shot of a still photograph hawking Bulova watches.

Ads became even more of a mass phenomenon, it played an important part in 20th-century history. During the World Wars, ads aggressively pitched everything from recruiting to war bonds, and cautioned against wasting resources, not to mention having loose lips.

Advertising also began to help to make individuals famous and build personal brands, whether through political advertising, movie and theater advertising, or even product advertising — such as a 1951 ad for Chesterfield cigarettes.

Meet the 'Mad Men'

Meanwhile, the companies that made ads were getting bigger — and more complex. The first business that specialized in creating and placing advertisements was launched in London in 1786, and the U.S. got its first in 1841, with Volney Palmer's firm in Philadelphia. But Palmer didn't actually create ads for his clients, just bought them space in newspapers.

America's ad agencies began to come into their own in the late 1800s — a few of today's biggest agencies can trace their roots back that far — and became powerhouses in the middle of the 20th century. And they got more cunning about crafting their products, leveraging survey research to shape campaigns and making aesthetic leaps. One of the innovators in this "Creative Revolution" was Bill Bernbach, whose sales pitches helped turn advertising into an art, such as the ad for the Volkswagen Beetle.

Show me the money

In 1776, the estimated total U.S. spending on advertising was $200,000. The figure hit $1 million just a quarter-century later, and crossed $1 billion in the early years of the 20th century. One estimate says U.S. ad spending in 2025 was just over $475 billion. (By contrast, the second-largest ad outlay last year, racked up by China, was just around $231 billion. Third place went to the U.K., at just under $62 billion.)

Advertising and its industry continue to transform. The internet brought a new era in ads, and now artificial intelligence is getting involved; the first AI-created ads started appearing last year.

Jef I. Richards is a professor emeritus of advertising and public relations at Michigan State University's College of Communication Arts and Sciences. He can be reached at reports@wsj.com.

data.title: IMF expects surge in loan requests as war drives energy shock
data.description:
The International Monetary Fund expects at least a dozen countries to seek new financial assistance as surging energy prices and supply chain disruptions linked to the Middle East conflict strain economies, Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva said on Wednesday.

Speaking during the IMF and World Bank spring meetings in Washington, Georgieva said demand for support could range between $20 billion and $50 billion, including both new loan programs and expansions of the IMF’s existing 39 financing arrangements.

She did not identify specific countries requesting aid but noted that several sub-Saharan African nations are among those seeking support.

Georgieva added that the IMF is not currently in discussions to expand Egypt’s $8 billion program, despite the war’s economic impact.

Supply disruptions likely to persist

Georgieva warned that disruptions stemming from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz could have lasting effects, even if the conflict ends quickly.

“These disruptions are not going to evaporate overnight, even if the war ends tomorrow,” she said, pointing to logistical constraints in global shipping.

“Because a tanker is a slow moving vessel, it would take 40 days to get all the way to Fiji. So we need to be prepared that the impact of the supply disruptions in the weeks ahead is going to be deeper.”

She highlighted risks to supply chains, particularly for Asian economies reliant on imports of oil, natural gas, naphtha, helium, and fertilisers from Gulf countries.

Growth outlook turns more cautious

The IMF has already signalled a deterioration in global economic conditions.

Its updated World Economic Outlook projected global growth of 3.1% for 2026, assuming a swift resolution to the conflict and declining oil prices.

However, IMF Chief Economist Pierre-Olivier Gourinchas said the global economy is now “drifting” toward a more adverse scenario.

Under this outlook, growth could slow to 2.5% in 2026, with oil prices averaging around $100 per barrel.

In a more severe scenario involving a prolonged conflict, global growth could fall to 2%, approaching recessionary conditions.

Separately, the IMF revised down its growth forecast for emerging market and developing economies to 3.9% in 2026, from 4.2% previously, citing rising energy and food prices and heightened uncertainty linked to the war.

The IMF noted that the impact of the conflict will vary across countries depending on their proximity to the region, energy dependence, trade linkages, and exposure to remittances.

Policy trade-offs intensify

The IMF warned that policymakers face increasingly difficult trade-offs as inflation rises while growth slows.

“The current hostilities in the Middle East pose immediate policy trade-offs: between fighting inflation and preserving growth and between supporting those affected by the rising cost of living and rebuilding fiscal buffers,” the IMF said in its World Economic Outlook update.

Georgieva urged governments to focus on reducing energy consumption and improving efficiency, suggesting measures such as incentivising lower fuel use or making public transport temporarily free.

Central banks urged to stay vigilant

On monetary policy, Georgieva advised central banks to remain alert to inflation risks while avoiding premature tightening.

“What we tell central banks is, if you have high credibility, signal that your objective is to protect price stability, but don't rush,” she said. “Wait to see how conditions would evolve.”

She added that central banks with less-established credibility may need to act more decisively, though she did not name specific countries.

data.title: ETOR Stock Jumps On $70 Million Crypto Wallet Deal, On Track For 3-Day Winning Streak
data.description:
Key points:
  • eToro Group shares edged higher following the announcement of a $70 million acquisition of crypto wallet provider Zengo.
  • The acquisition aims to enhance eToro's offerings by integrating Zengo's keyless wallet technology, improving user safety, and easing the management of crypto assets.
  • The Securities and Exchange Commission also approved a plan to remove day-trading limits for investors, which could lead to an increase in day trading volumes for smaller account traders to trade more actively, according to Goldman Sachs.

Shares of eToro Group Ltd. (ETOR) rose more than 6% on Wednesday, after the firm announced that it would buy Zengo, a crypto wallet provider, for $70 million.

The company said it wants to go beyond trading and into on-chain infrastructure and self-custody solutions. The partnership introduces Zengo's keyless wallet technology, which is based on multi-party computation (MPC), into eToro's ecosystem. This makes it safer and easier for users to keep crypto assets.

Zengo, which started in 2018, is a full-service crypto wallet that lets you swap tokens, stake them, and use decentralized apps. The connection could provide eToro users with more access to new ways to use cryptocurrency, like tokenized assets, decentralized trading, and products that make money.

The acquisition fits with eToro's long-term plan to connect the old financial world with the crypto-native economy, but users won't see any changes right away. 

ETOR Stock On Track For Three-Day Rally

eToro’s stock was trading over $36 in afternoon trade, on track for a three-day rally which has led the stock back to three-month highs. On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around ETOR improved to ‘bullish’ from the ‘bearish’ zone, while chatter around it rose to ‘high’ from the ‘normal’ zone over the past day. 

In addition to the new partnership, the uptick in ETOR’s stock came after the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) approved a plan to remove day-trading limits for investors. According to Goldman Sachs, this could lead to an increase in day trading volumes for smaller account traders to trade more actively.

Meanwhile, Citizens JMP reportedly lowered its price target on ETOR’s stock to $85 from $90 but kept a ‘Market Outperform’ rating.The analyst said large-cap financials remain durable, despite mounting valuation concerns. In contrast, he cited retail brokers and wealth platforms as examples of recent sell-offs that appear overdone in terms of fundamentals.

The firm used Robinhood (HOOD) as an example, stating that the stock has declined about 50% in six months despite reporting 52% revenue growth and remaining profitable.
